macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna: Purchase of Dovecote Offspring and the Requirement to Leave the First Brood

micro_segments.title: Overview of Mishna 80a: Buying the offspring of dovecotes and beehives
Details of dovecote purchase: leaving the first brood to maintain the mother
Beehive offspring and sterilization of remaining bees after taking three swarms
Honey loaves left behind for bee sustenance
Olive trees: leaving branches for regrowth

macro_segments.title: Gemara Debate on Dovecote Generations: First and Second Brood Explained

micro_segments.title: Baraita vs. Mishna: first brood versus first and second brood
Rav Kahana's resolution: distinguishing between mother and daughter broods across generations
Expansion of generations: why daughters need their own offspring to stay anchored
Gemara challenge: why daughters need offspring when they have mother and mate
Resolution: mothers are anchored by children; daughters need their own children

macro_segments.title: Beehive Swarms and the Meaning of Mesares: Sterilization vs. Alternation

micro_segments.title: Tannaitic teaching on three swarms and mesares; Rav Yehuda and Shmuel on mustard sterilization
Alternate view: bees eat mustard then consume honey instead of reproducing
Rabbi Yochanan's reading: mesares means alternation, not sterilization
Baraita clarification: three swarms taken consecutively, then alternation begins

macro_segments.title: Status of Honey in the Hive: Food vs. Liquid and Tumah Classifications

micro_segments.title: Rav Kahana: honey in hive never loses food status; never becomes liquid
Tosafos distinction: honey in wax is food, not liquid; differs from extracted honey
Gemara challenge: Baraita states honey in hive is neither food nor liquid
Abaye's resolution: statement applies only to the two loaves left for bees
Rava's attribution: teaching reflects Rabbi Eliezer's view of honey as ground-attached

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer on Honey: Ground Status, Shabbat Prohibition, and Tumah Rules

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer: honey in hive treated as ground-attached like fruit on tree
Pruzbul writing permitted; honey cannot become tamei while in hive
One who removes honey on Shabbat transgresses; like picking fruit from tree
Proof from Rabbi Elazar: verse comparison of yarot devash to forest growth

macro_segments.title: Honey Separation and Intent: When Extracted Honey Becomes Food or Liquid

micro_segments.title: Baraita: separated honey is neither food nor liquid until intent established
Intent to eat makes it food; intent to drink makes it liquid for tumah
Abaye: consistent with two-loaves exception; normal honey is auto-food
Rava: Baraita reflects Rabbi Eliezer; intent needed after ground-separation
Rav Zevid and Rav Acha: honey flowing onto disgusting vessel or wood chips

macro_segments.title: Consensus on Honey Status and Final Baraita Teaching

micro_segments.title: Teaching aligned with Rav Kahana: honey in hive auto-becomes food without intent
Summary reconciliation: normal honey is food; two-loaves exception requires intent

macro_segments.title: Olive Trees and Sycamores: Cutting Heights and Regrowth Standards

micro_segments.title: Baraita: purchaser cuts tefach above ground; allows regrowth for seller
Virgin sycamore requires three tefachim; once-cut requires two tefachim
Reeds and grapevines: cut above the knot; date palms and cedars uprootable

macro_segments.title: Sycamore Cutting Debate: Shevi'is Prohibition and Shabbat-Era Interpretations

micro_segments.title: Gemara challenge: virgin sycamore forbidden on Shevi'is as avodah
Rabbi Yehuda: normal cutting forbidden; permits only high or flush cuts
Abaye's range theory: three tefachim optimal for growth; flush or high cuts prevent it
Shevi'is vs. sale: Shevi'is requires zero growth; sale requires good regrowth

macro_segments.title: Cedar Trees and Growth: Resolving Contradictory Teachings on Regrowth

micro_segments.title: Baraita states cedars do not regrow; Gemara challenges with textual proof they do
Rabbi Chiya bar Lulina's drash: cedar and palm compared to righteous person
Resolution: non-cedar arz species do not regrow; true cedar does
Ten types of arz listed: cedar, acacia, myrtle, pine, juniper, cypress, and others
Baraita adds three additional arz types: pistachio, oak, coral, or laurel, plane, coral

honey status in hive (food vs. liquid)
Rabbi Eliezer on honey as ground-attached (karka)
